nodes.title: Build a fresh result array
nodes.why: Using a new array keeps the input unchanged and makes the function behavior predictable.
nodes.alternative: Mutate the original array in place.
nodes.tradeoff: Safer and easier to reason about, with extra memory for the copied output.

nodes.title: Flatten only when the current item is an array
nodes.why: This enforces one-level flattening: arrays are opened once, non-arrays are kept as-is.
nodes.tradeoff: Matches the problem exactly, but won’t flatten nested arrays beyond one level.

nodes.title: Spread array elements into the result
nodes.why: Spread inserts each child element directly, so one nesting layer disappears.
nodes.alternative: Loop through `el` and push each item manually.
nodes.tradeoff: Compact and readable, though explicit loops can be clearer to some readers.

nodes.title: Use built-in `flat` with depth 1
nodes.why: Depth `1` tells JavaScript to flatten only one layer, which matches this task.
nodes.alternative: Write a manual loop with `Array.isArray` checks.
nodes.tradeoff: Very concise, but depends on built-in behavior and interview constraints may vary.

nodes.title: Return the flattened result directly
nodes.why: No extra state is needed since `flat` already returns the final array.
nodes.alternative: Store in a variable first, then return.
nodes.tradeoff: Shorter code, but less room for inline debugging statements.

nodes.title: Keep non-array values unchanged
nodes.why: `flat(1)` only unwraps array children and leaves regular elements untouched.
nodes.alternative: Filter or transform values during flattening.
nodes.tradeoff: Predictable flatten behavior, but no built-in customization per element.
